items.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
items.correct: They are carried in the bloodstream and act on cells with specific receptors for that hormone.
items.misconception: Hormones are sent through nerves directly to specific organs.
items.random_wrong: Hormones are released directly into target organs by being injected by glands.
items.why: Students conflate nervous and endocrine signalling; they think every signal in the body travels through nerves.

items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: How does myelination increase the speed of nerve impulse conduction?
items.correct: Action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction), skipping the myelinated sections.
items.misconception: Myelin makes the axon thicker so the signal travels faster through more material.
items.random_wrong: Myelin contains additional ion channels that speed up depolarisation.
items.why: Students think 'more material = faster' rather than understanding that myelin INSULATES so the impulse can leap between gaps (saltatory).
The answer examiners want is: Reliability. “Validity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is an example of a control variable in an enzyme experiment?
items.why: Correct answer: pH. “Rate of reaction” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
